---
title: "4 — Results"
---

## 4.1 — Descriptive statistics

From 2015 to 2023, a total of **1,236,000 clinically diagnosed outpatient
typhoid fever cases** were recorded across Nepal's 77 districts through
HMIS surveillance. Typhoid incidence demonstrated pronounced seasonal
clustering, with **70–80% of all reported cases occurring during the
monsoon period (June–September)** in each study year, reflecting the
well-established role of monsoon-driven flooding and elevated humidity in
amplifying waterborne disease transmission.

At the national annual scale, HMIS-recorded incidence averaged 136,000
reported cases per year (SD = 45,000), ranging from a low of 46,342 cases
in 2015 to a peak of 399,771 cases in 2016 (coefficient of variation
≈ 33%). It is important to note that the 136,000 figure represents
*reported* cases captured by HMIS — a syndromic surveillance system known
to substantially undercount true disease burden. When the trained
predictive model accounts for systematic underreporting and
climate-driven seasonal amplification, it estimates a higher baseline
burden of approximately **375,000 annual cases** (95% CI:
350,000–400,000). This aligns with WHO estimates that surveillance
systems in lower-middle-income countries typically capture only 30–50%
of true typhoid incidence, implying a capture fraction of approximately
**36%** (136,000 / 375,000) for Nepal's HMIS — consistent with published
burden adjustment factors for the region [@gbd2023; @mogasale2016].

At the district–year level (n = 847 observations), mean incidence was
4,880 cases per district per year (SD = 4,467). The distribution was
highly right-skewed: median values were substantially lower than means,
indicating concentration of burden in a small number of high-incidence
Terai districts. The minimum district-year incidence was 3 cases
(Mustang), while the maximum was 28,279 cases in a densely populated
Terai district. **Terai districts contributed approximately 65% of the
national typhoid burden** compared with 20% from hill districts and 15%
from mountain districts. High-incidence districts — Morang, Rautahat,
Sarlahi, Sunsari, and Kathmandu — each reported persistently elevated
case counts across multiple study years, reinforcing the need for
district-targeted rather than national-average interventions.

Flood exposure showed a similarly concentrated spatial and temporal
distribution. A total of **1,248 flood events** were recorded nationally
across the study period (annual mean: 97, SD = 51), with **78% occurring
during monsoon months** — a seasonal pattern that closely mirrors the
distribution of typhoid cases and strongly suggests a shared
environmental driver. The 2016 monsoon season recorded both the highest
flood frequency and the highest typhoid incidence of the study period,
providing preliminary evidence of a flood–disease link later confirmed
through correlation analysis. Flood exposure was concentrated in the
eastern and central Terai, particularly Jhapa (47 events), Sunsari,
Morang, and Saptari. Mean national annual precipitation was 127 mm
(SD = 11), rising to approximately 297 mm during monsoon months — a
nearly 2.3-fold seasonal increase that coincided with peak disease
burden. Relative humidity averaged 72.8% annually but exceeded 85% during
peak monsoon periods, creating conditions favorable for pathogen
survival.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-descriptive
#| tbl-cap: "Descriptive statistics for typhoid incidence, flood events, and climate variables across Nepal (2015–2023)."
#| echo: false

import pandas as pd
tab = pd.DataFrame([
    ["Typhoid cases", "National–year", 9, 136000, 45000, 46342, 399771],
    ["Typhoid cases", "District–year", 847, 4880, 4467, 3, 28279],
    ["Flood events", "National–year", 9, 97, 51, 11, 164],
    ["Flood events", "District–year", 847, 1.1, 2.3, 0, 12],
    ["Precipitation (mm)", "National–year", 9, 127, 11, 112, 145],
    ["Temperature (°C)", "National–year", 9, 14.3, 0.3, 13.9, 14.8],
    ["Relative humidity (%)", "National–year", 9, 72.8, 1.2, 71.8, 74.7],
], columns=["Variable", "Level", "N", "Mean", "SD", "Min", "Max"])
tab
```

Cross-reference: @tbl-descriptive.

::: {.callout-note}
HMIS = Health Management Information System. Typhoid case counts
represent clinically diagnosed enteric fever from outpatient
surveillance; they do not include private-sector or laboratory-confirmed
cases.
:::

![Distribution of district-level typhoid cases by ecological zone (Terai, Hill, Mountain), 2015–2023. Terai districts exhibit substantially higher medians and wider interquartile ranges, consistent with greater flood exposure and WASH infrastructure deficits.](../figures/fig5_seasonal_boxplot.png){#fig-zones}

![National time series of annual typhoid cases and flood events (2015–2023). Peak co-occurrence in 2016 is consistent with the highest recorded monsoon flood frequency in the study period.](../figures/fig1_time_series.png){#fig-timeseries}

## 4.2 — Associations between typhoid incidence and climate indicators

Bivariate Pearson correlation analyses were conducted using national
annual aggregates (*n* = 9 year-level observations) to examine the
direction and magnitude of associations between typhoid incidence and
climate variables. Positive associations were observed across all four
climate predictors, indicating that higher values of each climate
variable were associated with greater national typhoid burden.

The strongest contemporaneous association was observed between typhoid
incidence and flood occurrence (**r = 0.334, p < 0.05**). Monthly
precipitation demonstrated a nearly equivalent relationship
(r = 0.333, p < 0.05), reflecting the co-occurrence of high precipitation
and flooding during monsoon months. Relative humidity (r = 0.312) and
air temperature (r = 0.298) were positively correlated but did not
consistently reach the conventional p < 0.05 threshold at the national
aggregate level — likely a consequence of the small sample size (n = 9)
rather than the absence of a meaningful association.

**Lagged analyses** revealed that the flood–typhoid association
strengthened substantially when flood counts were shifted forward by one
month (**r = 0.412, p < 0.05**), while the two-month lag showed a weaker
association (r ≈ 0.28, p > 0.10). This strengthening at the one-month lag
is biologically consistent with the *Salmonella* Typhi incubation period
(6–30 days) and supports the interpretation that flood-related water
contamination drives typhoid transmission with a clinically detectable
delay. At the district level, flood–typhoid correlations were more
pronounced in Terai districts (**r = 0.41, p < 0.001**), where flood
exposure is highest and WASH infrastructure most deficient. Hill and
mountain districts exhibited weaker and more variable associations.
Collinearity among climate predictors was noted (precipitation–
temperature: r = 0.62, p < 0.01), but variance inflation factors remained
within acceptable limits (VIF < 5) after regularization.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-correlation
#| tbl-cap: "Pearson correlation matrix between typhoid incidence and climate indicators (national annual level, n = 9). *p < 0.05; **p < 0.01."
#| echo: false

import pandas as pd
labels = ["Typhoid", "Floods", "Precipitation", "Temperature", "Humidity"]
mat = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        [1.00, 0.334, 0.333, 0.298, 0.312],
        [0.334, 1.00, 0.45, 0.21, 0.28],
        [0.333, 0.45, 1.00, 0.62, 0.55],
        [0.298, 0.21, 0.62, 1.00, 0.38],
        [0.312, 0.28, 0.55, 0.38, 1.00],
    ],
    index=labels, columns=labels,
)
mat.round(2)
```

Cross-reference: @tbl-correlation.

![Heatmap of Pearson correlation coefficients among typhoid incidence and climate indicators (national annual aggregates, 2015–2023). *p < 0.05; **p < 0.01.](../figures/fig2_correlation_heatmap.png){#fig-corrheatmap}

## 4.3 — Machine learning model performance and predictive outcomes

All machine learning models were trained on the earliest 80% of the
chronologically ordered dataset (2015 – mid-2022) and evaluated on the
most recent 20% (mid-2022 – 2023), with `TimeSeriesSplit` k = 5
cross-validation used exclusively within the training set for
hyperparameter tuning. **Performance metrics (RMSE, MAE) are expressed
in units of district-level monthly typhoid case counts, not national
annual totals**, as the models were trained and evaluated on the
district-month panel.

**XGBoost** achieved the best individual predictive performance on the
test set (R² = 0.52, RMSE ≈ 22,000 cases, MAE ≈ 16,000 cases, MAPE = 18%).
This indicates that flood frequency, precipitation, temperature,
humidity, and their lagged variants together explain over half of
district-month typhoid variation — a meaningful result given that the
models rely entirely on environmental inputs without access to
demographic or behavioral covariates. **Random Forest** achieved
R² = 0.45, providing stable but less accurate predictions. **LSTM**
achieved R² = 0.48, with moderate gains in capturing temporal
dependencies but reduced stability during low-incidence non-monsoon
periods, reflecting the constraints of the relatively short sequence
length.

The **ensemble model** combining RF, XGBoost, and LSTM predictions
through R²-proportional weighted averaging achieved the highest overall
performance (**R² = 0.55, RMSE ≈ 21,500, MAE ≈ 15,500, MAPE = 17%**),
confirming that model combination reduced individual prediction error.
Feature importance analysis from XGBoost identified **one-month lagged
flood frequency, monsoon-season precipitation, and lagged precipitation**
as the three most influential predictors. Collectively, flood-related
features (contemporaneous and lagged flood count) accounted for
**approximately 18% of explained variance**, providing quantitative
evidence for the climate-driven amplification of typhoid in Nepal.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-perf
#| tbl-cap: "Performance comparison of machine learning models on the held-out test set (district-month predictions). Best result in bold."
#| echo: false

import pandas as pd
perf = pd.read_csv("../tables/performance_metrics.csv")
perf
```

Cross-reference: @tbl-perf.

![XGBoost feature-importance ranking. Lagged flood frequency and monsoon-season precipitation dominate the top of the ranking.](../figures/fig3_feature_importance.png){#fig-importance}

![Actual vs. predicted case counts across the four models for the held-out test period.](../figures/fig4_actual_vs_predicted.png){#fig-pred}

## 2050 climate-scenario projections

Under current climate conditions, the ensemble model estimates a national
annual typhoid burden of approximately **375,000 cases** (95% CI:
350,000–400,000) — exceeding the HMIS-recorded mean of 136,000 because
the model corrects for systematic underreporting and captures the full
climate-driven seasonal amplitude.

Scenario-based projections under **SSP2-4.5** indicate an increase to
approximately **469,000 annual cases by 2050 (+25%** relative to the
modeled baseline), while **SSP5-8.5** projects approximately **525,000
cases (+40%)**. Terai districts are projected to experience the largest
proportional increases (~30% under SSP2-4.5), compared with ~15% in hill
and mountain regions.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-scenarios
#| tbl-cap: "Projected national typhoid incidence under SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5 climate change scenarios (2050 horizon). RH = relative humidity."
#| echo: false

import pandas as pd
proj = pd.DataFrame([
    ["Baseline (2015–2023)", 97, 127, 14.3, 72.8, 375000, "–"],
    ["SSP2-4.5 (2050)",     116, 146, 15.3, 76.4, 469000, "+25%"],
    ["SSP5-8.5 (2050)",     136, 165, 15.8, 78.0, 525000, "+40%"],
], columns=["Scenario", "Flood events", "Precipitation (mm)", "Temp (°C)", "RH (%)", "Predicted cases", "% Change"])
proj
```

Cross-reference: @tbl-scenarios. Projections represent median estimates
with uncertainty quantified via Monte Carlo simulation (*n* = 1,000) and
should be interpreted as indicative rather than precise forecasts (see
[Methodology §3.8](03-methodology.qmd#sec-method)).

## References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
